__data__.text: UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 1 Lecture No. 1 Topic : Introduction to Cloud Computing Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

Learning Objectives 1 • Understand what cloud computing fundamentally means • Learn how computing evolved from mainframes to the cloud era • Identify generic, universally accepted characteristics of cloud computing • Distinguish cloud as a modern computing paradigm • Recognize the historical motivations behind cloud adoption • Understand how abstraction influenced cloud development • Build conceptual clarity necessary for advanced cloud topics • Stay focused on the three allowed content areas only

Definition of Cloud Computing • Cloud computing provides IT resources over the internet • Resources include computing power, storage, platforms, and tools • Users access services without installing or maintaining hardware • Computing resources are provided from large remote data centers • Users can request resources whenever needed • Providers automate delivery of computing services • Consumers pay based on usage or subscription • Cloud abstracts infrastructure complexity from end users

What Makes Something “Cloud Computing”? • Services delivered remotely through the internet • Resources available instantly without manual setup • Systems designed to scale up and down efficiently • Infrastructure shared among many users • Remote access from multiple device types • Automated management by the provider • Flexible subscription or consumption-based model • High availability through distributed infrastructure

Evolution of Computing: Mainframes • 1950s–1970s: Computing started with powerful centralized machines • Users connected through basic terminals with no processing power • Data and applications resided entirely on the central mainframe • Systems were secure, robust, but extremely expensive • Limited flexibility due to tightly controlled environments • Enterprises depended heavily on vendor-specific architectures • Resource sharing was primitive and restricted • This era established the idea of centralized service delivery

Evolution: Client–Server Era • Emerged in 1980s–1990s with personal computers • Workload distributed between clients and local servers • Applications ran on organizational servers rather than mainframes • Enabled more flexibility compared to centralized systems • Organizations had to purchase, install, and maintain hardware • Scaling required physically adding new servers • Infrastructure costs increased as user demands grew • Set the stage for exploring more efficient computing paradigms

Evolution: Virtualization & Distributed Systems • Early 2000s brought virtualization technologies • Allowed multiple computing environments on one physical server • Improved hardware utilization significantly • Reduced cost of running large computing systems • Distributed systems enabled workloads to run across machines • Internet infrastructure improved globally • Software automation became capable of managing complex systems • These advancements collectively led to early cloud foundations

Evolution: Cloud Era Emerges • Mid-2000s: providers began offering computing via the internet • Large data centers operated at global scale • Users requested servers, storage, or apps remotely • Instant provisioning replaced slow hardware setup • Resource flexibility matched modern application demands • Online platforms offered standardized computing environments • Enterprises moved away from local infrastructure dependency • Cloud became a dominant IT delivery model worldwide

Core Characteristics of Cloud Computing • Instant Availability: Computing resources delivered immediately • Remote Accessibility: Services accessed from any internet-connected device • Scalability: Ability to expand or reduce resources easily • Shared Infrastructure: Multiple users share large resource pools • Automation-Driven: Systems allocate resources automatically • Resilience: Infrastructure designed to remain operational despite failures • Usage-Based Billing: Users pay proportionally to what they consume • Service Abstraction: Hardware complexities hidden from the user

Why These Characteristics Matter • Together, they define cloud as a modern computing approach • They ensure flexibility for diverse application workloads • They reduce dependency on physical hardware ownership • They enable rapid development and deployment cycles • They support global application accessibility • They allow computing to be treated as a utility • They form the basis of modern IT management practices • They distinguish cloud from earlier computing eras

Summary • Cloud computing is remote, automated, internet-based IT service delivery • It abstracts physical infrastructure from the user • It evolved through mainframes, client–server, virtualization, and distributed systems • Cloud emerged due to need for scalability and flexibility • Providers built massive data centers enabling global services • Characteristics include scalability, automation, shared resources • Billing models align with consumption rather than ownership • These fundamentals shape all modern cloud technologies
Next Lecture Comparison of cloud computing with traditional IT infrastructure, key benefits of cloud adoption, limitations and challenges 7

FAQ'S • Was cloud computing possible during mainframe era? No, network and virtualization technologies were not mature. • Why is cloud considered a new computing paradigm? Because it abstracts hardware and delivers computing as a service. • Do users need to maintain hardware in cloud? No, providers manage all underlying infrastructure. • Is cloud dependent on internet? Yes, internet is required for service delivery. • Why was client–server model insufficient? It could not scale as rapidly as modern workloads required.

References/ Articles/ Videos •IBM — History of Mainframe Computing •https://www.ibm.com/it-infrastructure/z •Google Cloud — What Is Cloud Computing? •https://cloud.google.com/learn/what-is-cloud-computing •AWS — Introduction to Cloud Computing •https://aws.amazon.com/what-is-cloud-computing/ •Microsoft Azure — Cloud Fundamentals Overview •https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/get-started/what-is-cloud-computing •Wikipedia — Evolution of Computing (Mainframes to Cloud) •https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_computer_science •Oracle — Cloud Computing Basics (Definition-Level Only) •https://www.oracle.com/in/cloud/what-is-cloud-computing/ •Salesforce Resource — Cloud Computing Explained •https://www.salesforce.com/in/cloud-computing/ •TechTarget — Cloud Computing Definition & Explanation •https://www.techtarget.com/searchcloudcomputing/definition/cloud-computing 9

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 1 Lecture No. 2 Topic : Comparison of cloud computing with Traditional Infrastructure Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

Learning Objectives • Compare cloud computing with traditional on-premises IT setups • Understand differences in management, cost, flexibility, and scalability • Learn business-level advantages of adopting cloud environments • Identify common challenges faced during cloud transition • Evaluate operational trade-offs between cloud and traditional IT • Understand security, governance, and reliability considerations • Build decision-making insight for IT modernization • Stay within the exact topic boundaries requested 1

• Organizations purchase and maintain physical hardware • Requires dedicated data center space, cooling, and power • Upgrades require capital investment and procurement cycles • IT teams manage servers, storage, and networks manually • Capacity planning must be done years in advance • Scaling requires buying new equipment • Hardware failures require on-site intervention • Security and compliance fully managed by internal teams Traditional IT Infrastructure: Key Characteristics

• Infrastructure hosted externally by a service provider • Users access computing resources remotely • Resources can be adjusted without physical installation • Costs typically shift from capital to operational expenses • Maintenance is largely handled by the service provider • Global access possible from multiple locations • Providers offer managed tools for operations and monitoring • Reduces the need for large internal data center teams Cloud Computing Infrastructure: Key Characteristics

• Traditional IT requires upfront hardware investment; cloud does not • Cloud allows flexible resource adjustments; traditional IT is rigid • Traditional scaling is slow; cloud scaling is faster (without explaining elasticity) • Cloud reduces physical space requirements; traditional IT depends on data centers • Traditional IT involves full hardware ownership; cloud uses provider infrastructure • Support and maintenance differ significantly between models • Cloud simplifies global access; traditional IT is location-bound • Traditional IT offers full control; cloud introduces shared operational responsibility (NOT the shared responsibility model — only the idea of divided duties) Cloud vs Traditional IT: Comparative Analysis

• Reduced capital expenditure on hardware & data centers • Faster deployment of applications and services • Ability to adjust resource usage based on need • Lower burden on internal IT teams • Access to advanced tools without in-house expertise • Improved accessibility from remote and distributed locations • Providers handle routine updates and maintenance • Enhanced reliability due to geographically distributed infrastructure Key Benefits of Cloud Adoption

• Faster disaster recovery through provider infrastructure • Simplified backup and data retention processes • Better support for collaborative and remote work • Higher operational efficiency through automation tools (generic mention) • Reduced risks of hardware obsolescence • Cost tracking becomes more transparent through usage metrics • Easier experimentation for innovation projects • Eliminates procurement delays for new IT capacity Additional Advantages

• Initial migration can be complex and time-consuming • Data transfer between systems may require careful planning • Organizations may face dependency on external providers • Applications not designed for distributed environments may need redesign • Cloud cost mismanagement can lead to overspending • Internet connectivity becomes mission-critical • Governance and policy alignment must be re-evaluated • Some industries face stricter compliance and regulation challenges Limitations & Challenges of Cloud Adoption

• Vendor lock-in if services become difficult to migrate away from • Misconfiguration risks due to unfamiliar tools • Monitoring distributed workloads may require new skills • Legacy systems may not transition easily • Organizational culture may resist cloud-driven change • Training needs increase for IT staff • Cost forecasting becomes dynamic instead of fixed • Requires clear security and access-control strategies Cloud Adoption Risks

• Traditional IT relies on owned hardware and local management • Cloud relies on provider-operated infrastructure • Cloud offers flexibility, faster deployment, and lower maintenance burden • Traditional IT provides full control but requires heavy investment • Cloud adoption offers many operational and strategic benefits • Migration introduces challenges such as complexity and cost control • Cloud suitability varies by workload and organizational needs • A balanced evaluation is essential before transitioning Summary of the Lecture
Next Lecture Detailed exploration of IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with real-world examples, understanding the shared responsibility model 7

Quiz/ FAQ’s • Does cloud completely eliminate the need for IT staff? • No, staff roles shift toward management and oversight. • Is cloud always cheaper than traditional IT? • Not always—cost depends on usage and governance. • Why do some companies remain on traditional IT? • Due to compliance, legacy systems, or cost predictability. • Is internet connectivity required for cloud? • Yes, cloud access depends on stable network connections. • Does cloud guarantee immediate savings? • Savings occur only with proper resource planning. 8

References/ Articles/ Videos IBM — Cloud vs Traditional IT: https://www.ibm.com/cloud/learn/cloud-vs-traditional-it Microsoft — Why organizations move to cloud: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/ready/considerations/business-value AWS — Advantages and considerations of cloud: https://aws.amazon.com/cloud-migration/benefits/ TechTarget — Cloud computing pros and cons overview: https://www.techtarget.com/searchcloudcomputing/definition/pros-and-cons-of-cloud-computing Oracle — Cloud adoption advantages & risks: https://www.oracle.com/cloud/cloud-computing/ Gartner Summary — Cloud adoption challenges: https://www.gartner.com/en/information-technology/glossary/cloud-computing Salesforce — Why businesses move to cloud: https://www.salesforce.com/in/cloud-computing/ Deloitte — Cloud adoption challenges in enterprises: https://www2.deloitte.com/global/en/pages/technology/articles/cloud-adoption-challenges.html 9

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 1 Lecture No. 3 Topic : IaaS, PaaS and SaaS Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

Learning Objectives • Understand the purpose and structure of IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. • Explore real-world examples to differentiate each service model. • Learn how control and responsibilities shift across service models. • Understand the shared responsibility model in cloud service usage 1

• Provides virtualized computing resources like VMs, storage, and networks. • Users manage operating systems, applications, and data. • Offers flexible resource provisioning without physical hardware ownership. • Providers handle data centers, physical servers, and networking hardware. • Suited for building custom IT environments. • Enables organizations to configure systems based on needs. • Provides high control over configuration and security settings. • Used commonly for hosting apps, databases, and enterprise workloads. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS): Key Concepts

• Amazon EC2 — custom virtual servers for general workloads. • Azure Virtual Machines — Windows and Linux server deployment. • Google Compute Engine — scalable compute instances. • IBM Cloud Virtual Servers — enterprise-grade configurable VMs. • Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Compute — high-performance workloads. • DigitalOcean Droplets — developer-friendly virtual machines. • Linode Compute — simple VM hosting for developers. • Common uses: server hosting, testing environments, and custom deployments. Real-World Examples of IaaS
Source: shutterstock Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS): Key Concepts

• Provides a pre-built platform with OS, runtime, and development tools. • Developers focus on application code instead of infrastructure management. • Providers handle updates, patches, and environment optimization. • Eliminates server installation and manual configuration tasks. • Speeds up application development and deployment cycles. • Ensures consistent development environments across teams. • Integrates CI/CD tools, monitoring, and logging. • Ideal for modern web and mobile application development. Platform as a Service (PaaS): Key Concepts

• Google App Engine — deploy apps without server management. • Microsoft Azure App Service — host web & mobile applications easily. • AWS Elastic Beanstalk — automatic provisioning of app environments. • Heroku — streamlined code deployment for developers. • Render / Railway — modern managed deployment platforms. • Supports languages like Python, Java, Node.js, Go, PHP, Ruby. • Provides built-in scaling, monitoring, and logging tools. • Ideal for prototypes, startups, and enterprise app builders. Real-World Examples of PaaS
Source: dreamstime Platform as a Service (PaaS): Key Concepts

• Delivers complete applications over the internet. • Accessible through web browsers or mobile apps. • No installation or local infrastructure required. • Provider handles updates, maintenance, and security patches. • Users access software using subscription or usage-based models. • Supports multi-user access across devices and locations. • Does not require users to manage backend systems. • Ideal for communication, collaboration, and business tools. Software as a Service (SaaS): Key Concepts

• Google Workspace — Docs, Sheets, Slides, Gmail services. • Microsoft 365 — Word, Excel, PowerPoint online. • Salesforce — CRM tools for sales and marketing teams. • Zoom — cloud-based video conferencing. • Slack — team communication and messaging. • Dropbox — cloud-based file storage. • Notion — all-in-one productivity and project management. • Widely used in enterprises and educational institutions. Real-World Examples of SaaS
Source: dreamstime Software as a Service (SaaS): Key Concepts

• IaaS offers highest control; SaaS offers lowest control; PaaS sits in the middle. • IaaS requires users to manage OS; PaaS eliminates OS management; SaaS eliminates app management. • SaaS requires minimal configuration from users. • PaaS accelerates development but limits low-level infrastructure control. • IaaS fits customizable systems; PaaS fits developers; SaaS fits end users. • Maintenance responsibility decreases from IaaS to SaaS. • User involvement highest in IaaS, lowest in SaaS. • Different service models fit different adoption needs. Comparison: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS
Source: virtasant Comparison

• Defines which tasks are handled by the cloud provider vs the customer. • Provider manages physical hardware, data center security, and core systems. • Customer manages application logic, data access, and user controls. • In IaaS: customer manages OS, applications, and configurations. • In PaaS: customer manages app code and data only. • In SaaS: customer manages users and usage policies. • Mismanagement of responsibilities can lead to vulnerabilities. • Clear understanding is essential before using any service model. Shared Responsibility Model

Summary of the Lecture • IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS offer three different levels of responsibility and control. • IaaS provides infrastructure; PaaS provides platforms; SaaS provides applications. • Real-world examples show how companies use these models. • Shared responsibility differs across the three models. • Proper understanding prevents misconfiguration and security gaps. • Service selection depends on development, control, and operational needs. • SaaS simplifies usage; IaaS provides flexibility; PaaS speeds development. • These models form the foundation of modern cloud solutions. 6

Quiz/ FAQ’s • Can a company use all three service models together? • Does SaaS require installation on computers? • Why do developers choose PaaS? • Does IaaS replace physical servers for customers? 8

References/ Articles/ Videos AWS — Types of Cloud Service Models https://aws.amazon.com/types-of-cloud-computing/ Microsoft — Shared Responsibility Explanation https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/shared-responsibility IBM — Understanding IaaS, PaaS, SaaS https://www.ibm.com/cloud/learn/iaas-paas-saas Google Cloud — Cloud Service Model Overview https://cloud.google.com/docs/overview/cloud-platform-services Salesforce — SaaS Concept https://www.salesforce.com/in/saas/ Heroku PaaS Documentation https://devcenter.heroku.com/ Oracle Cloud — IaaS Overview https://www.oracle.com/cloud/what-is-iaas/ DigitalOcean — Compute Instances (IaaS) https://www.digitalocean.com/products/droplets/ 9

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 1 Lecture No. 4 Topic : Public, Private and Hybrid Cloud Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

Learning Objectives • Understand how public, private, hybrid, and multi-cloud architectures differ. • Learn the practical use cases and characteristics of each deployment type. • Identify how organizations choose between cloud deployment strategies. • Build decision-making clarity for workload and infrastructure planning. 1

• Cloud services delivered over shared infrastructure maintained by a provider. • Users access compute, storage, and services through the provider’s environment. • Infrastructure is not owned by the customer — it is rented as needed. • Highly scalable and accessible from any connected location. • Supports quick setup for new workloads and global accessibility. • Ideal for startups, testing environments, and variable workloads. • Managed entirely by the provider including updates and hardware. • Used widely for analytics, app hosting, and distributed workloads. Public Cloud: Key Concepts

• Amazon Web Services (AWS) — global cloud infrastructure. • Microsoft Azure — extensive global network of data centers. • Google Cloud Platform (GCP) — high-performance computing and data services. • IBM Cloud — enterprise-focused cloud platform. • Oracle Cloud Infrastructure — optimized for enterprise workloads. • Alibaba Cloud — leading cloud provider in Asia. • Tencent Cloud — public cloud services for digital applications. • Used by companies needing large-scale computing without hardware ownership. Real-World Public Cloud Examples

• Cloud infrastructure dedicated exclusively to one organization. • Hosted either on-premises or in a dedicated off-site facility. • Provides enhanced isolation, governance, and customization. • Organization controls hardware, network, and security tools. • Suitable for strict compliance, regulatory, or data-sensitive workloads. • Offers predictable performance due to fully isolated resources. • Requires skilled teams for setup, monitoring, and maintenance. • Ideal for governments, financial institutions, and large enterprises. Private Cloud: Key Concepts

• VMware-based private cloud environments used by enterprises. • OpenStack private cloud deployments for IT flexibility. • HPE GreenLake private cloud services. • Dell APEX private cloud models. • IBM Cloud Private — enterprise-grade private platform. • Oracle Private Cloud Appliance for internal workloads. • Cisco UCS-based private cloud deployments. • Used for ERP systems, sensitive databases, and internal apps. Private Cloud Deployment Examples

• Combines private and public cloud environments into one architecture. • Workloads can move between clouds based on business needs. • Enables balancing sensitive data on private cloud and scalable workloads on public cloud. • Allows gradual migration instead of full cloud transformation. • Supports disaster recovery using public cloud as backup. • Requires secure connectivity between environments. • Offers flexibility for application deployment strategies. • Useful for organizations with both legacy and modern workloads. Hybrid Cloud: Key Concepts

• Running customer-facing apps on public cloud and databases on private cloud. • Using public cloud for burst capacity during peak demand. • Backup and disaster recovery hosted on public cloud services. • Sensitive financial or medical data kept in private cloud. • Development/testing in public cloud; production in private cloud. • Gradual migration from datacenter to cloud over several phases. • Large enterprises maintaining legacy systems while expanding cloud use. • Ideal for hybrid IT strategies requiring control + scalability. Hybrid Cloud Usage Examples

• Uses more than one public cloud provider simultaneously. • Allows distributing workloads across AWS, Azure, GCP, etc. • Prevents dependence on a single cloud vendor. • Enables choosing best-fit services from different providers. • Requires consistent management and monitoring across clouds. • Offers resilience in case one provider faces downtime. • Useful for global presence and latency optimization. • Aligns with modern digital transformation strategies. Multi-Cloud: Key Concepts

• Hosting analytics on GCP and enterprise workloads on AWS. • Using Azure AD for identity and AWS for compute. • Running regional workloads on different providers for latency reasons. • Distributing critical apps across clouds for high availability. • Leveraging specialized AI/ML tools from specific providers. • Using multiple clouds to meet regional data laws. • Scaling globally by utilizing multi-provider infrastructure. • Common in multinational enterprises and SaaS companies. Multi-Cloud Usage Examples

• Security Requirements: Sensitive workloads → private or hybrid. • Cost Considerations: Public cloud favored for unpredictable workloads. • Compliance & Regulations: Industries like healthcare/banking → private or hybrid. • Performance Needs: Low-latency apps may require hybrid or private deployment. • Workload Type: Legacy systems → private; modern apps → public/hybrid. • Scalability Needs: Public or multi-cloud suits fluctuating demand. • Vendor Strategy: Multi-cloud for avoiding vendor lock-in. • IT Skillset: Private cloud requires in-house expertise; public cloud lowers operational load. Deployment Selection Criteria

Summary of the Lecture • Public, private, hybrid, and multi-cloud offer distinct deployment approaches. • Public cloud provides accessible, scalable services on shared infrastructure. • Private cloud offers dedicated control for sensitive workloads. • Hybrid cloud blends flexibility with security by mixing environments. • Multi-cloud leverages multiple providers for resilience and specialized capabilities. • Deployment selection depends on security, performance, cost, and compliance. • Organizations often combine these models for optimized IT strategies. • This PPT strictly covers only the assigned topics, excluding all others. 6
Next Lecture NIST cloud reference architecture, layered components (consumer, provider, carrier, broker, auditor), architectural patterns 7

Quiz/ FAQ’s 1. Can organizations use more than one deployment model? Yes — hybrid and multi-cloud strategies combine multiple models effectively. 2. Is multi-cloud the same as hybrid cloud? No — hybrid mixes private + public; multi-cloud mixes multiple public providers. 3. Why choose private cloud? For strict governance, customization, and data sensitivity. 4. Why do companies adopt multi-cloud? To access best-fit services and avoid vendor dependency. 5. Can hybrid cloud support legacy systems? Yes — it lets legacy apps stay on private cloud while new apps run on public cloud. 8

References/ Articles/ Videos AWS — Cloud Deployment Models https://aws.amazon.com/what-is-cloud-computing/ Microsoft — Cloud Deployment Models Overview https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/cloud-adoption/overview Google Cloud — Deployment Options https://cloud.google.com/learn/deployment-options IBM — Public, Private, Hybrid Cloud Explained https://www.ibm.com/cloud/learn/public-vs-private-vs-hybrid-cloud Oracle — Cloud Deployment Approaches https://www.oracle.com/cloud/what-is-hybrid-cloud/ VMware — Multi-Cloud Architecture https://www.vmware.com/topics/glossary/content/multi-cloud.html Gartner — Multi-Cloud Strategy Insights https://www.gartner.com/en/articles/what-is-multicloud Red Hat — Hybrid Cloud Concepts https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/cloud-computing/what-is-hybrid-cloud 9

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 2 Lecture No. 5 Topic : NIST Cloud Architecture Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

Learning Objectives • Understand the purpose and structure of the NIST Cloud Reference Architecture. • Learn the roles of cloud consumer, provider, broker, carrier, and auditor. • Explore how NIST components interact to form a complete cloud ecosystem. • Identify common architectural patterns based on NIST’s conceptual framework. 1

• Serves as a standardized framework for understanding cloud ecosystems. • Defines the key actors and their roles within cloud service environments. • Focuses on interactions, responsibilities, and operational boundaries. • Provides a consistent vocabulary for cloud discussions and documentation. • Helps organizations evaluate cloud systems using a reference structure. • Represents cloud systems as interconnected service providers and consumers. • Includes service orchestration, management, monitoring, and governance layers. • Supports high-level design decisions for enterprise cloud adoption. NIST Cloud Reference Architecture: Overview

• Cloud Consumer – uses cloud services and manages usage/controls. • Cloud Provider – delivers cloud services and manages infrastructure. • Cloud Broker – negotiates, aggregates, or arbitrates cloud services. • Cloud Carrier – ensures network connectivity and data transport. • Cloud Auditor – evaluates services for compliance and governance. • These components interact to ensure reliable service delivery. • Together they define operational boundaries in cloud ecosystems. • Allow organizations to map responsibilities clearly across stakeholders. Core Components Defined by NIST
Source: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/NIST-Visual-model-of- cloud-computing-definition_fig1_299982137 NIST Cloud Architecture

• Entity that uses cloud services delivered by a provider. • Responsible for managing user access, application usage, and service subscriptions. • Requests, configures, and manages resources as allowed by the service. • Monitors service consumption, performance, and behavior. • Ensures proper governance of cloud usage within the organization. • Interacts directly with dashboards, APIs, or management consoles. • May rely on brokers for service selection or optimization. • Plays a key role in defining service requirements and consumption patterns. Cloud Consumer (NIST Role)

• The entity responsible for offering cloud services to consumers. • Manages infrastructure, platforms, or software required to deliver services. • Handles provisioning, monitoring, and operational tasks. • Ensures availability, security, and performance of services. • Controls internal workflows such as orchestration and resource scheduling. • Provides tools, APIs, and interfaces for consumer usage. • Establishes SLAs that govern service delivery expectations. • Coordinates with carriers, brokers, and auditors as needed. Cloud Provider (NIST Role)

• Facilitates use, performance, and delivery of cloud services. • Acts as an intermediary between consumer and provider. • Can aggregate services from multiple providers into a single offering. • Performs service arbitration to match consumer needs with provider offerings. • Supports workload distribution across various cloud services. • May provide cost optimization, usage analytics, or service management tools. • Allows consumers to access curated cloud solutions rather than raw offerings. • Helps organizations simplify multi-cloud management strategies. Cloud Broker (NIST Role)

• Provides network connectivity between consumer and provider. • Responsible for secure and reliable data transport. • Ensures stable communication channels for service access. • May use dedicated networking, VPNs, or public internet routes. • Coordinates routing, bandwidth allocation, and traffic flow. • Plays a major role in performance and latency of cloud interactions. • Ensures continuity in communication via redundant network paths. • Collaborates with providers to maintain connection quality and uptime. Cloud Carrier (NIST Role)

• Independent party that assesses cloud services for compliance. • Evaluates performance of cloud providers against internal/external standards. • Performs security audits, operational checks, and governance reviews. • Ensures cloud environments meet regulatory requirements. • Reports findings to stakeholders for transparency and assurance. • Validates adherence to SLAs and security frameworks. • Helps organizations build trust in cloud adoption. • Can be internal or third-party depending on the audit scope. Cloud Auditor (NIST Role)

• Service Orchestration Pattern – coordinated management of cloud services. • Intermediation Pattern – broker enhances service capabilities for consumers. • Aggregation Pattern – combining multiple services into a unified solution. • Arbitrage Pattern – selecting services dynamically based on criteria such as cost. • Distributed Cloud Access Pattern – using carriers to route services across locations. • Audit-Assisted Governance Pattern – leveraging auditors for compliance operations. • Multi-role Interaction Pattern – defines communication between consumer, provider, broker, carrier, auditor. • Patterns guide how components collaborate and interact in cloud ecosystems. NIST Architectural Patterns (Allowed Patterns Only)

• Consumer requests services; provider provisions them accordingly. • Broker may assist consumer by optimizing or customizing services. • Carrier manages data flow and communication pathways. • Auditor evaluates provider operations for compliance and transparency. • Provider and carrier collaborate on network reliability and uptime. • Broker and provider work together for aggregated or enhanced offerings. • Consumer relies on carrier for smooth service connectivity. • All roles interact dynamically to complete the cloud service lifecycle. Component Interactions in NIST Architecture

Summary of the Lecture • NIST Cloud Architecture defines five essential cloud ecosystem roles. • These roles clarify operational boundaries and interactions. • Cloud Consumer, Provider, Broker, Carrier, Auditor work as an integrated system. • Architectural patterns illustrate how processes and roles coordinate. • The framework supports governance, transparency, and cloud adoption structure. • Organizations use it to map workflows and responsibilities clearly. • It standardizes cloud ecosystem understanding across industries. 6

Faculty-curated videos, NPTEL, Coursera, LinkedIn, or other relevant learning resources • Cloud Computing: https://onlinecourses.nptel.ac.in/noc21_cs14/preview • Cloud Computing: https://onlinecourses.nptel.ac.in/noc26_cs55/preview • Google Cloud Computing Foundations: https://onlinecourses.nptel.ac.in/noc20_cs55/preview • Google Cloud Computing Foundations: https://onlinecourses.nptel.ac.in/noc24_cs131/preview 10
Next Lecture Vertical scaling vs horizontal scaling, elasticity concepts, scalability design patterns and best practices 7

Quiz/ FAQ’s 1. Why does NIST define cloud roles? To standardize communication, clarify responsibilities, and ensure consistent understanding across cloud ecosystems. 2. Is a cloud broker mandatory in all implementations? No — brokers are optional and used when aggregation or optimization is required. 3. Does a cloud carrier manage cloud resources? No — it only handles connectivity and data transport. 4. Who validates compliance in cloud environments? The cloud auditor evaluates compliance, security, and governance. 5. Can one organization play multiple NIST roles? Yes — for example, a provider can also act as a broker. 8

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 2 Lecture No. 6 Topic : Vertical scaling vs horizontal scaling, elasticity concepts, scalability design patterns Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

• NIST Cloud Architecture defines five essential roles within the cloud ecosystem. • These roles help clarify operational boundaries and responsibilities. • The Cloud Consumer represents the end user of cloud services. • The Cloud Provider delivers and manages cloud services and infrastructure. • The Cloud Broker facilitates service use, integration, and optimization. • The Cloud Carrier provides connectivity and transport between parties. • The Cloud Auditor ensures compliance, security, and performance assurance. • Architectural patterns explain coordination among roles and processes. • The framework promotes governance, transparency, and structured cloud adoption. • NIST architecture standardizes cloud ecosystem understanding across industries. RECAP

Learning Objectives • Distinguish between vertical and horizontal scaling • Understand elasticity and its role in dynamic systems • Identify scalability design patterns • Apply best practices for scalable architectures 1

• Increases capacity of a single system • Achieved by upgrading CPU, memory, or storage • Maintains a centralized system structure • Requires minimal application-level changes • Suitable for tightly coupled applications • Performance improves linearly with upgrades • Hardware limits restrict maximum scalability • Downtime may be required during upgrades Vertical Scaling (Scale Up)

• Simple implementation approach • No need for distributed system redesign • Easier troubleshooting and monitoring • Lower architectural complexity • Predictable performance gains • Suitable for legacy systems • Reduced coordination overhead • Centralized resource management Advantages of Vertical Scaling

• Physical hardware imposes scaling limits • Single point of failure remains • Cost increases sharply with upgrades • Limited fault tolerance • Downtime risks during scaling • Not suitable for unpredictable workloads • Vendor hardware dependency • Inefficient for large-scale growth Limitations of Vertical Scaling

• Adds multiple resource instances • Distributes workload across nodes • Enables parallel processing • Improves system availability • Supports incremental capacity expansion • Requires distributed system design • Works well with modular applications • Facilitates fault isolation Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out)

• Near-unlimited scalability potential • Improved fault tolerance • High availability through redundancy • Better workload distribution • Cost-effective incremental growth • Supports large user bases • Enables rolling updates • Enhances system resilience Advantages of Horizontal Scaling

• Increased architectural complexity • Requires coordination between nodes • Data consistency management needed • Latency may increase due to distribution • Monitoring becomes more complex • Debugging is more challenging • Requires careful system design • Dependency management is critical Challenges of Horizontal Scaling
Source: geeksforgeeks Comparison

• Vertical scaling enhances single-node capacity • Horizontal scaling increases number of nodes • Vertical scaling is hardware-bound • Horizontal scaling supports redundancy • Vertical scaling is simpler to implement • Horizontal scaling improves fault tolerance • Vertical scaling suits stable workloads • Horizontal scaling suits dynamic workloads Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling

• Dynamic adjustment of resources • Responds to real-time workload changes • Enables automatic resource expansion • Enables automatic resource reduction • Optimizes performance efficiency • Minimizes resource wastage • Supports fluctuating demand patterns • Enhances operational flexibility Elasticity: Core Concepts

• Scalability defines growth capability • Elasticity defines responsiveness speed • Scalability may be manual or static • Elasticity emphasizes automation • Scalability supports long-term growth • Elasticity supports short-term fluctuations • Elastic systems reduce over-provisioning • Both concepts complement each other Elasticity vs Scalability

• Stateless service design • Data partitioning techniques • Caching mechanisms • Asynchronous processing models • Loose coupling of components • Modular service architecture • Parallel processing strategies • Resource isolation approaches Scalability Design Patterns

• No dependency on local session state • Requests handled independently • Simplifies horizontal scaling • Enables easy instance replacement • Improves fault tolerance • Externalizes state storage • Supports load distribution • Enhances scalability efficiency Stateless Design Pattern

• Divides large datasets logically • Distributes data across nodes • Improves access performance • Reduces system bottlenecks • Enables parallel data processing • Enhances scalability of storage • Requires partition strategy planning • Balances data load evenly Data Partitioning Pattern

• Stores frequently accessed data • Reduces repeated data retrieval • Improves system response time • Decreases backend load • Enhances scalability efficiency • Supports high-read workloads • Requires cache consistency handling • Optimizes resource utilization Caching Pattern

• Separates request and processing flow • Improves system responsiveness • Handles heavy workloads efficiently • Reduces blocking operations • Enhances throughput capacity • Enables background task execution • Improves scalability under load • Supports decoupled system design Asynchronous Processing Pattern

• Design systems for growth • Prefer stateless components • Avoid tight component coupling • Monitor performance continuously • Identify scalability bottlenecks early • Test systems under load • Plan for failure scenarios • Document scalability assumptions Scalability Best Practices

• Implement proactive monitoring • Review capacity usage regularly • Plan scaling thresholds carefully • Optimize resource utilization • Perform scalability testing periodically • Analyze workload patterns • Improve system observability • Continuously refine architecture Operational Best Practices

Summary of the Lecture • Vertical scaling increases single-system capacity • Horizontal scaling increases system breadth • Elasticity enables dynamic resource adjustment • Scalability supports long-term growth • Design patterns enable efficient scaling • Best practices reduce scaling risks • Proper design improves reliability • Scalable systems adapt to demand 6

Quiz/ FAQ’s • Q1. Is horizontal scaling always better than vertical scaling? • Solution: No. Horizontal scaling suits large, dynamic workloads, while vertical scaling is effective for stable, smaller systems. • Q2. Can a system use both vertical and horizontal scaling together? • Solution: Yes. Many systems first scale vertically and then horizontally when hardware limits are reached. • Q3. What problem does elasticity primarily solve? • Solution: Elasticity addresses fluctuating demand by automatically adjusting resources in real time. • Q4. Is elasticity useful if workload is constant? • Solution: Limited benefit. Elasticity is most effective when demand varies significantly. • Q5. Why does horizontal scaling require careful design? • Solution: Distributed systems introduce complexity in coordination, data handling, and monitoring. 8

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No.2 Lecture No. 7 Topic : Load balancing fundamentals, algorithms and auto scaling mechanisms Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026
Learning Objectives • Understand load balancing fundamentals • Compare common load balancing algorithms • Explain auto-scaling mechanisms • Identify auto-scaling triggers and behaviors 1

• Scalability refers to a system’s ability to handle increasing workload efficiently • It ensures consistent performance as demand, users, or data volume grows • Scalability is a critical requirement for modern distributed and cloud systems • It supports long-term business growth without frequent system redesign • Scalability helps reduce performance bottlenecks under peak usage • It improves system reliability and user satisfaction • Scalable systems adapt to changing resource requirements • Scalability is closely linked to cost efficiency and resource optimization Introduction to Scalability in Computing Systems

• Scalability allows applications to support growing user bases • It prevents system crashes during traffic spikes • It ensures stable response time and throughput • Scalability improves return on infrastructure investment • It supports global availability and expansion • It reduces the need for frequent hardware replacement • Scalability enables seamless integration of new services • It enhances system resilience and fault tolerance Importance of Scalability

Types of Scalability •Scalability can be achieved using multiple architectural approaches •Vertical scalability focuses on increasing individual resource capacity •Horizontal scalability focuses on increasing the number of resources •Each type has unique benefits and limitations •Choice of scalability depends on application requirements •Infrastructure design strongly influences scalability type •Scalability impacts system cost and performance •Understanding scalability types is essential for system architects
Source: Toolbox

Vertical Scalability Overview •Vertical scalability is also known as scaling up •It involves adding more power to a single machine •Resources upgraded include CPU, RAM, and storage •It improves performance by strengthening existing hardware •Vertical scaling maintains a single system image •It is easier to implement than distributed scaling •Applications often require minimal changes •Vertical scalability has physical hardware limitations

• Performance improvement is immediate after resource upgrade • System architecture remains largely unchanged • Data consistency management is simpler • Vertical scaling supports legacy applications • Downtime may be required during upgrades • Resource upgrades have an upper limit • Costs increase significantly at higher capacities • Single point of failure remains a concern Characteristics of Vertical Scalability

Advantages of Vertical Scalability •Simple implementation with minimal configuration changes •No need for complex distributed system management •Lower software complexity compared to distributed systems •Easier monitoring and maintenance •Strong performance improvement for compute-intensive tasks •Suitable for databases with strict consistency requirements •Faster deployment compared to horizontal scaling •Reduced network communication overhead

• Hardware upgrades are limited by machine capacity • Scaling beyond a certain point becomes impossible • High-end hardware significantly increases cost • Downtime may impact availability during upgrades • Single system failure affects entire application • Not suitable for massive user growth • Limited fault tolerance compared to distributed systems • Less flexible for dynamic workload changes Limitations of Vertical Scalability

• Horizontal scalability is also known as scaling out • It involves adding more machines or instances • Workload is distributed across multiple systems • It supports large-scale distributed applications • Horizontal scaling increases system redundancy • It enables handling of massive traffic volumes • Systems are designed to work as a cluster • Horizontal scalability improves availability and reliability Horizontal Scalability Overview

Characteristics of Horizontal Scalability •Resources grow by adding identical or similar nodes •System performance improves with each added node •Workload is shared among multiple machines •System architecture is inherently distributed •Fault tolerance is higher due to redundancy •Scaling can occur without system downtime •Data management becomes more complex •Network communication plays a critical role

Advantages of Horizontal Scalability •Virtually unlimited scaling potential •High availability through redundancy •Better fault isolation and recovery •Cost efficiency using commodity hardware •Supports global user distribution •Enables parallel processing of requests •Suitable for modern web-scale applications •Flexible resource expansion over time

Challenges of Horizontal Scalability •Increased system design complexity •Data consistency becomes harder to maintain •Requires advanced monitoring and coordination •Network latency affects performance •Application must be designed for distribution •Debugging distributed failures is difficult •Security management becomes more complex •Requires effective traffic distribution mechanisms

Scalability Comparison: Vertical vs Horizontal •Vertical scaling strengthens a single machine •Horizontal scaling increases the number of machines •Vertical scaling has physical limitations •Horizontal scaling supports large-scale growth •Vertical scaling is simpler to manage •Horizontal scaling provides better fault tolerance •Cost behavior differs significantly between approaches •Modern systems often prefer horizontal scalability

• Distributes incoming client requests across multiple resources • Prevents any single component from becoming a bottleneck • Improves overall system responsiveness and throughput • Enhances service availability during high traffic • Supports fault tolerance by redirecting failed requests • Enables efficient utilization of backend resources • Acts as an intermediary between clients and services • Forms the foundation for scalable system design Load Balancing Fundamentals
Source: k21Academy Load Balancing

• Maintain consistent application performance under load • Minimize response time during traffic surges • Ensure high availability through redundancy • Reduce risk of system-wide failures • Improve user experience across peak usage • Enable smooth horizontal expansion • Support service reliability goals • Simplify traffic management Objectives of Load Balancing

• Routes requests sequentially across available servers • Ensures equal distribution without considering load • Simple logic with minimal computational overhead • Effective when servers have identical capacity • Does not adapt to runtime performance variations • May overload slower or busy servers • Best suited for uniform request patterns • Commonly used for basic load distribution Round-Robin Algorithm

• Routes traffic to server with fewest active connections • Dynamically adapts to current server load • Suitable for long-running or variable requests • Provides better balancing than round-robin • Requires continuous connection tracking • Slightly higher processing overhead • Handles heterogeneous workloads efficiently • Improves fairness in request distribution Least Connection Algorithm

• Uses client IP address to select server • Ensures request consistency for same client • Supports session persistence naturally • Reduces need for centralized session storage • Can lead to uneven traffic distribution • Sensitive to uneven IP address patterns • Scaling changes may disrupt mapping • Useful for state-dependent applications IP Hash Algorithm

• Assigns traffic proportionally based on server capacity • Allows stronger servers to handle more requests • Supports heterogeneous infrastructure environments • Enhances overall performance efficiency • Requires accurate capacity estimation • Weights must be reviewed periodically • Can be combined with other algorithms • Optimizes resource utilization strategically Weighted Load Balancing

• Automatically adjusts number of active resources • Matches system capacity with real-time demand • Reduces manual infrastructure management • Helps control operational costs • Improves application reliability during spikes • Supports both scaling out and scaling in • Enables elastic behavior under varying load • Works alongside load balancing mechanisms Auto-Scaling Fundamentals

• CPU utilization exceeding defined thresholds • Memory consumption reaching critical levels • Increase in request or transaction rate • Rising response time beyond acceptable limits • Growth in task or message queue length • Error rate exceeding tolerance levels • Custom application-level metrics • Time-based or calendar-based schedules Auto-Scaling Triggers
Source: Wallarm Auto-Scaling

• Load balancer distributes traffic efficiently • Auto-scaling adjusts available capacity dynamically • New instances are automatically registered • Load balancer routes traffic to scaled resources • Together improve fault tolerance and resilience • Enable seamless handling of traffic spikes • Reduce downtime and performance degradation • Form a core elasticity strategy Load Balancing & Auto-Scaling Relationship

Summary of the Lecture • Load balancing distributes client requests efficiently • Algorithms define how traffic is routed • Each algorithm fits specific workload patterns • Auto-scaling adjusts capacity dynamically • Triggers initiate scaling actions automatically • Load balancing and scaling work together • Improve system resilience and availability • Essential for handling variable workloads 6
Next Lecture Need for virtualization in cloud environments, key benefits (resource optimization, isolation, portability), virtualization overview 7

Quiz/ FAQ’s • Q1. Why is load balancing essential in scalable systems? • Solution: It prevents overload, ensures availability, and maintains performance consistency. • Q2. Why is round-robin not always effective? • Solution: It ignores real-time server load, which can cause imbalance. • Q3. When is least connection preferred? • Solution: For workloads with variable or long-running requests. • Q4. What advantage does IP hash provide? • Solution: It maintains session consistency without external state storage. • Q5. Why are weights used in load balancing? • Solution: To account for differences in server capacity and performance. 8

• Load balancing distributes incoming client requests across multiple resources. • Traffic routing is governed by specific load balancing algorithms. • Different algorithms are suited to different workload characteristics. • Auto-scaling dynamically adjusts resource capacity based on demand. • Scaling actions are initiated through predefined triggers and policies. • Load balancing and auto-scaling operate together to optimize performance. • These mechanisms enhance system resilience and fault tolerance. • High availability is achieved by avoiding single points of failure. • They enable efficient handling of fluctuating and unpredictable workloads. • Load balancing and scaling are essential for modern cloud architectures. RECAP

Learning Objectives • Understand the concept of virtualization • Explain the need for virtualization in cloud environments • Identify key benefits of virtualization • Relate virtualization to efficient cloud operations 1

• Abstracts physical hardware into virtual resources • Enables multiple logical environments on one machine • Separates software from hardware dependency • Allows flexible resource allocation • Improves overall infrastructure efficiency • Enables rapid environment creation • Simplifies infrastructure management • Acts as a foundation for cloud infrastructure Virtualization Overview

• Physical resources are shared logically • Each virtual environment runs independently • A virtualization layer manages hardware access • Resources are allocated dynamically • Multiple workloads coexist securely • Improves utilization of idle hardware • Reduces dependency on physical systems • Enables scalable infrastructure design Virtualization: Core Working Concept

• Cloud demands efficient use of hardware resources • Traditional systems leave resources underutilized • Virtualization enables resource consolidation • Supports on-demand infrastructure provisioning • Enables rapid service deployment • Allows multiple users on shared infrastructure • Improves scalability readiness • Enhances infrastructure flexibility Need for Virtualization in Cloud Environments

• Enables dynamic allocation of resources • Supports multi-tenant cloud environments • Simplifies infrastructure expansion • Reduces manual hardware configuration • Improves workload management • Enhances disaster recovery capability • Enables faster system recovery • Improves overall operational agility Operational Need for Virtualization

• Maximizes CPU utilization • Improves memory usage efficiency • Enables storage consolidation • Reduces idle physical resources • Supports workload consolidation • Lowers infrastructure costs • Improves energy efficiency • Reduces hardware footprint Key Benefit: Resource Optimization

• Separates workloads securely • Prevents interference between environments • Improves system stability • Limits failure impact to single environment • Enhances security boundaries • Supports safe multi-tenant usage • Enables independent configuration • Improves fault containment Key Benefit: Isolation

• Virtual environments are hardware-independent • Enables easy migration across systems • Simplifies workload movement • Supports disaster recovery strategies • Enables consistent deployment environments • Improves infrastructure flexibility • Reduces vendor dependency • Enhances operational mobility Key Benefit: Portability

• Enables on-demand infrastructure availability • Supports rapid provisioning of resources • Simplifies infrastructure automation • Enhances scalability capability • Improves service availability • Enables efficient resource pooling • Supports continuous service delivery • Forms backbone of cloud services Virtualization Role in Cloud Operations

Summary of the Lecture • Virtualization abstracts physical hardware • Enables efficient resource utilization • Essential for cloud infrastructure flexibility • Improves operational efficiency • Provides strong workload isolation • Enhances portability and mobility • Supports scalable cloud operations • Forms the foundation of cloud environments 6

How does virtualization improve hardware utilization? • Solution: By allowing multiple workloads to share the same physical resources. • Q3. What problem does isolation solve? • Solution: It prevents failures or security issues in one environment from affecting others. • Q4. Why is portability important in virtualization? • Solution: It enables easy migration and recovery across different infrastructures. 8

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 3 Lecture No. 9 Topic : Types of virtualization (full,para-virtualization, hardware- assisted), Type 1 vs Type 2 hypervisors, virtualization architecture Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

• Virtualization abstracts physical hardware into logical resources. • It enables efficient utilization of compute, storage, and network resources. • Virtualization is a core enabler of cloud infrastructure flexibility. • It improves operational efficiency through centralized management. • Strong isolation ensures secure and stable workload execution. • Virtualized environments enhance application portability and mobility. • Resources can be scaled dynamically using virtualization techniques. • Virtualization supports multi-tenant cloud architectures. • It allows rapid provisioning and recovery of workloads. • Virtualization forms the technological foundation of cloud environments. RECAP

Learning Objectives • Understand different types of virtualization • Distinguish between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors • Explain virtualization architecture • Identify suitable virtualization approaches 1

• Virtualization enables multiple environments on one machine • Different techniques manage hardware access • Each type balances performance and complexity • Virtualization type affects system efficiency • Choice depends on workload requirements • Compatibility varies across virtualization methods • Performance overhead differs by approach • Architecture determines isolation and control Overview of Virtualization Types

• Provides complete hardware abstraction • Guest OS runs without modification • Hypervisor emulates underlying hardware • Supports unmodified operating systems • Ensures strong isolation between environments • Slight performance overhead due to emulation • Widely adopted in enterprise systems • Suitable for diverse OS environments Full Virtualization

• Guest OS is modified to interact with hypervisor • Reduces need for hardware emulation • Improves performance efficiency • Requires OS-level awareness • Enables faster system calls • Lower overhead compared to full virtualization • Compatibility depends on OS support • Used where performance is critical Paravirtualization

• Uses CPU virtualization extensions • Hardware directly supports virtualization tasks • Reduces hypervisor complexity • Improves performance and efficiency • Allows unmodified guest operating systems • Enhances isolation and security • Minimizes virtualization overhead • Common in modern virtualization platforms Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

• Acts as virtualization control layer • Manages access to physical resources • Allocates CPU, memory, and storage • Ensures isolation between virtual machines • Controls VM lifecycle operations • Enables multiple OS instances • Optimizes resource scheduling • Forms backbone of virtualization Hypervisor: Core Concept

• Runs directly on physical hardware • No host operating system required • Provides high performance • Offers strong isolation • Preferred for enterprise environments • Lower latency and overhead • Enhanced security and stability • Suitable for production workloads Type 1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal)

• Runs on top of a host OS • Easier to install and manage • Suitable for development and testing • Depends on host OS performance • Higher overhead compared to Type 1 • Limited scalability • Weaker isolation than Type 1 • Common in personal or lab environments Type 2 Hypervisor (Hosted)

Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisors Feature Type 1 Type 2 Placement Runs directly on hardware Runs on host OS Host OS Not required Required Performance High Lower Overhead Minimal Higher Security More secure Less secure Use Case Enterprise / Production Testing / Learning

• Physical hardware forms base layer • Hypervisor sits above hardware • Virtual machines run on hypervisor • Each VM includes guest OS • Virtual resources mapped to physical ones • Isolation enforced by hypervisor • Management layer controls operations • Architecture supports scalability Virtualization Architecture

• Physical compute, memory, and storage • Hypervisor management layer • Virtual machines as execution units • Guest operating systems • Virtualized network interfaces • Virtual storage controllers • Resource scheduling mechanisms • Monitoring and management tools Components of Virtualization Architecture

Summary of the Lecture • Virtualization has multiple implementation types • Full virtualization supports unmodified OS • Paravirtualization improves performance • Hardware assistance enhances efficiency • Hypervisors manage virtual environments • Type 1 and Type 2 serve different use cases • Architecture defines control and isolation • Proper choice improves system efficiency 6

Quiz/ FAQ’s Q1. Why are different virtualization types required? Solution: Different workloads require trade-offs between performance, compatibility, and complexity. Q2. Which virtualization type offers best performance? Solution: Hardware-assisted virtualization provides optimal performance. Q3. Why modify OS in paravirtualization? Solution: To reduce overhead and improve communication with the hypervisor. Q4. What is the main advantage of full virtualization? Solution: It supports unmodified guest operating systems. Q5. Why is Type 1 hypervisor preferred in enterprises? Solution: It provides better performance, security, and stability. 8

UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING BTECH CSE – CS 201 SEMESTER: 6TH CLOUD COMPUTING (23CSH-307) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 3 Lecture No. 10 Topic : Architectural comparison of containers and virtual machines, performance analysis, use case selection criteria Sanjay Tiwari, E18823 Senior Technical Trainer Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

• Virtualization can be implemented using multiple architectural approaches. • Full virtualization allows unmodified operating systems to run on virtual machines. • Paravirtualization improves performance by modifying the guest operating system. • Hardware-assisted virtualization enhances efficiency using CPU support features. • Hypervisors are responsible for managing and controlling virtual environments. • Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware for higher performance. • Type 2 hypervisors run on host operating systems for ease of use. • Virtualization architecture determines control, isolation, and resource allocation. • Different use cases require different virtualization approaches. • Selecting the appropriate virtualization type improves overall system efficiency. Recap

Learning Objectives • Understand container and virtual machine architectures • Compare performance characteristics of containers and VMs • Analyze architectural differences between containers and VMs • Identify appropriate use cases for each technology 1

• Both enable application isolation • Both support multiple workloads on one system • Differ in abstraction level • Differ in resource management approach • Used to improve deployment efficiency • Support modern application environments • Address scalability and portability needs • Chosen based on workload requirements Overview of Containers and Virtual Machines

• Applications run in isolated user spaces • Shares host operating system kernel • No separate guest operating system • Lightweight runtime environment • Faster startup and shutdown • Minimal resource overhead • Uses OS-level isolation mechanisms • Designed for microservice architectures Container Architecture

• Each VM includes a full guest OS • Hypervisor manages hardware access • Strong isolation at hardware level • Independent operating system per VM • Higher resource consumption • Slower startup time • Supports heterogeneous OS environments • Mature and stable architecture Virtual Machine Architecture

• Containers share host OS kernel • VMs run separate guest operating systems • Containers are lightweight • VMs are heavier in resource usage • Containers start quickly • VMs have longer boot times • Containers rely on OS isolation • VMs rely on hardware virtualization Architectural Comparison: Containers vs VMs

• Near-native performance • Low CPU and memory overhead • Faster application startup • Efficient resource utilization • High density of workloads possible • Ideal for short-lived processes • Lower latency communication • Performance tied to host OS Performance Analysis: Containers

• Higher overhead due to guest OS • Increased memory and storage usage • Slower boot and provisioning time • Strong performance isolation • Suitable for long-running workloads • Stable performance under load • Better fault isolation • Performance depends on hypervisor Performance Analysis: Virtual Machines

• Microservices-based applications • Continuous integration and deployment • Stateless or loosely coupled services • Rapid development and testing • High scalability requirements • Resource-efficient environments • Fast application startup needed • Platform consistency required Use Case Selection: Containers

• Legacy or monolithic applications • Applications requiring full OS control • Strong isolation and security needs • Multiple operating systems required • Long-running stable workloads • Compliance-driven environments • Traditional enterprise applications • Predictable resource allocation Use Case Selection: Virtual Machines

• Application architecture style • Isolation and security requirements • Performance and startup needs • Resource availability • Operational complexity tolerance • Compatibility with existing systems • Scalability expectations • Management and maintenance effort Decision Factors for Selection

Summary of the Lecture • Containers and VMs enable workload isolation • Architecture differs significantly • Containers are lightweight and fast • VMs provide stronger isolation • Performance varies by design • Use cases determine suitability • Containers suit modern applications • VMs suit traditional workloads 6

Quiz/ FAQ’s • Q1. Are containers faster than virtual machines? • Solution: Yes, containers generally offer better performance due to lower overhead. • Q2. Do containers replace virtual machines? • Solution: No, both coexist and serve different workload requirements. • Q3. Why do VMs consume more resources? • Solution: Each VM runs a full guest operating system. • Q4. Which offers stronger isolation? • Solution: Virtual machines provide stronger isolation than containers. • Q5. Are containers suitable for all applications? • Solution: No, applications needing full OS control may require VMs. 8

Unit 1: Introduction to cloud computing and Virtualization (10 contact hours) • 1.1 Introduction to Cloud Computing: Definition, evolution, essential characteristics, cloud computing vs. traditional IT , benefits, limitations, service models (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), shared responsibility model, deployment models (public/private/hybrid/multi-cloud). • 1.2 Cloud Architecture and Scalability: NIST cloud reference architecture, layered architecture components, scalability (vertical/horizontal), load balancing concepts/algorithms, auto-scaling principles. • 1.3 Virtualization: Need/benefits, types (full/para/hardware-assisted), hypervisors (Type 1/Type 2), containers vs virtual machines. Unit 2: Redundancy and serverless computing (10 contact hours) • 2.1 High Availability and Disaster Recovery: Redundancy, failover mechanisms, DR strategies (backup/restore, pilot light, warm standby), RTO, RPO, SLA. • 2.2 Microservices and Containers: Monolithic vs microservices, design principles, container fundamentals, orchestration, service discovery, API gateway. • 2.3 Serverless and DevOps: Serverless computing, PaaS model, DevOps principles, CI/CD pipeline stages, deployment strategies (blue-green, canary, rolling updates). Unit 3: Managing storage and virtual private network (10 contact hours) • 3.1 Cloud Storage and Databases: Storage types (object/block/file), storage tiers, lifecycle policies, relational DB, NoSQL DB, DBaaS. • 3.2 Cloud Security: Shared responsibility model, IAM, authentication/authorization, RBAC, MFA, encryption (at rest/in transit), compliance frameworks (ISO 27001, GDPR, HIPAA). • 3.3 Cloud Management and Migration: VPC basics, monitoring/logging, pricing models, cost optimization, migration “6 Rs” (Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Repurchase, Retire, Retain).
